name: Sonanta
classification_notes: The page content is minimal but the name and foundry context suggest a text serif; classified as transitional serif pending more detailed specimen information.
summary: A serif typeface from Patio Foundry offered in Regular and Regular Italic.
description: Sonanta is a serif from Patio Foundry with at least a Regular and Regular Italic style. The page provides limited specimen detail, showing sentence, paragraph, alphabet and numeral views. Its scope appears modest, suited to text-setting tasks.
foundry_blurb: Patio Foundry offer Sonanta with a playable specimen showcasing Regular and Regular Italic across sentence, paragraph, alphabet and numeral views.

name: Sliza
classification_notes: An expressive display typeface with deliberately broken angles and hand-cut paper aesthetics, drawing from mid-century collage and cut-and-paste graphic traditions rather than conventional geometric or grotesque construction.
summary: A display typeface built on the aesthetic of hand-cut paper and mid-century collage, citing Saul Bass as its primary reference.
description: Sliza draws from the cut-and-paste visual language of Saul Bass, using deliberately broken angles and informal edges to evoke mid-century graphic energy. The design moves away from rigid geometry in favour of raw, dynamic forms that suggest physical collage rather than digital construction. Custom expressive ligatures reinforce this character, introducing sharper connections and unexpected shapes while maintaining legibility.
foundry_blurb: Patio Foundry describe Sliza as a display font channelling the bold, mid-century brilliance of Saul Bass, built on the raw, dynamic energy of hand-cut paper and collage. Its deliberately broken angles and informal edges are designed for maximum impact and a genuine vintage feel, with custom ligatures that enhance specific letter combinations and reinforce the font's cut-and-paste character.

name: Xanky
classification_notes: Minimal page content makes precise sub-classification difficult, but the condensed variant and the name suggest a display-oriented typeface; classified as display given the limited weights and the foundry's boutique, expressive output.
summary: A single-weight display typeface from Patio Foundry, available in both regular and condensed cuts.
description: Xanky offers two width variants from a single weight: a standard cut and a condensed version suited to tighter settings and headlines. The page provides little contextual detail, but the pairing of widths suggests a focus on flexible display use. Patio Foundry is a small independent operation, and Xanky fits the profile of a tightly scoped, character-driven release.
foundry_blurb: Patio Foundry offer Xanky in two cuts: Xanky Regular and Xanky Regular Condensed.

name: MARKSY
classification_notes: An all-caps headline typeface with contextual character alternates to introduce organic rhythm; built specifically for display use with no text-setting intent.
summary: An all-caps display typeface that uses contextual character alternates to break the mechanical rigidity typical of uppercase-only headline faces.
description: Built from individual uppercase letterforms, MARKSY introduces multiple versions of select characters that swap in contextually, producing subtle variation across a headline without sacrificing typographic consistency. Two weights, Regular and Bold, keep the offering direct and focused on display applications. The alternates do the heavy lifting where a purely mechanical all-caps face would read as inert.
foundry_blurb: Patio Foundry describe MARKSY as a headline typeface constructed from individual uppercase letters. To counter a rigid or mechanical appearance, certain characters have multiple versions that alternate contextually, introducing organic visual rhythm while keeping the face firmly in display territory.

name: Rubica
classification_notes: The naming conventions and preview weights suggest a clean, geometric or humanist sans-serif family; without further classification detail from the page, a geometric sans is the most plausible subcategory given the minimal naming and contemporary foundry context.
summary: A four-weight geometric sans from Patio Foundry, running from Thin through to Bold.
description: Rubica offers four weights spanning Thin, Light, Regular and Bold, covering the most common range for editorial and branding work. The foundry page provides limited detail beyond the weight range, suggesting a restrained, purpose-built family rather than an expansive superfamily. Suited to clean, minimal design contexts where a straightforward sans is needed without excess variation.
foundry_blurb: Patio Foundry offers Rubica as a purchasable typeface with four weights: Thin, Light, Regular and Bold, presented with standard size and style preview options.

name: Jift
classification_notes: Described explicitly as a block typeface with heavy uniform strokes, geometric construction, rectangular terminals and minimal stroke contrast, built from modular square and rectangular forms for display impact.
summary: A single-weight block display typeface built from modular square and rectangular forms with minimal stroke contrast and rectangular terminals.
description: Jift is constructed on a strictly modular grid, using squares and rectangles as its building blocks to achieve a heavy, mechanical appearance. Stroke contrast is minimal throughout, and the consistent vertical stress keeps letterforms uniform and direct. Suited to display settings where impact and legibility at size are the priority.
foundry_blurb: Patio Foundry describe Jift as a block typeface characterised by heavy uniform strokes and geometric construction, designed to maximise legibility and impact in display contexts. They note its minimal stroke contrast, rectangular terminals and consistent vertical stress, with letterforms built from basic shapes such as squares and rectangles to produce a strong, mechanical appearance.

name: BITRUSH
classification_notes: The naming conventions BITRUSH DOT and BITRUSH SQUARE, along with the overall branding, point clearly to a pixel or bitmap-inspired display typeface with geometric, tech-inflected construction.
summary: A pixel-inspired display family from Patio Foundry, split into Dot and Square variants built on a hard geometric grid.
description: BITRUSH comes in two subfamilies, DOT and SQUARE, each working from a pixel or bitmap visual language suited to screen-forward and tech-adjacent design contexts. The geometric construction gives it a retro-digital character without feeling strictly nostalgic. Best suited to headlines, logotypes and short-form display work where a hard, grid-based aesthetic is called for.
foundry_blurb: Patio Foundry offer BITRUSH in two variants, BITRUSH DOT and BITRUSH SQUARE, presented as a pixel-style display typeface available to purchase directly from the foundry.
